paper_metadata.title: Public acceptability of carbon pricing: Unravelling the impact of revenue recycling
paper_metadata.geographic_scope: Multi-country systematic review (studies mainly from Europe, North America, plus selected countries in Asia, Latin America, Africa and Oceania)
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: Systematic PRISMA review of 48 empirical studies (surveys, experiments, qualitative work) on public attitudes to carbon and fuel taxation, 2004–March 2023.
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Synthesizes how different revenue recycling designs and related factors (fairness, effectiveness, trust, information, experience) shape public acceptability, support and willingness to pay for carbon and fuel taxes across 48 empirical studies.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Environmental earmarking of carbon tax revenues
determinants.definition: Using carbon or fuel tax revenues for environmental or climate-related purposes instead of the general budget or non-environmental spending.
determinants.mechanism: Creates a visible link between the tax and environmental benefits, increases perceived effectiveness and fairness, and alleviates distrust about misuse of revenues, thereby boosting acceptance.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon and fuel tax proposals where revenues are earmarked for environmental or climate expenditures (e.g. green funds) rather than general budget use.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support/WTP for carbon or fuel taxes under environmental earmarking versus alternative revenue uses.
determinants.evidence_quote: “Substantial and robust evidence in the literature indicates that environmental and climate earmarking can significantly enhance public acceptability across various contexts.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Ranks highest among all revenue options in the consensus ordinal ranking of reviewed studies.
determinants.notes: Effect can be sizable but acceptability may still remain limited in absolute terms.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Subsidies to low-carbon technologies and energy efficiency
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues for subsidies to low-carbon technologies and energy efficiency
determinants.definition: Earmarking carbon/fuel tax revenues to subsidize renewable energy, clean technologies, and household or firm energy-efficiency investments.
determinants.mechanism: Households see direct, tangible environmental and financial benefits from cleaner technologies, increasing perceived effectiveness and fairness of the tax relative to its costs.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon and fuel tax designs where revenues are recycled as subsidies for renewables, clean technology adoption and energy-efficiency measures.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support/WTP for carbon or fuel taxes with revenues used for low-carbon and efficiency subsidies.
determinants.evidence_quote: “Subsidies to low-carbon technologies (e.g. renewable energy) and energy efficiency have emerged as the most popular in many studies… and among the most popular ones in many others.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Joint top position in consensus ordinal ranking (score 8).

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues for environmental infrastructure investments
determinants.definition: Earmarking carbon/fuel tax revenues for infrastructure such as public transport, cycling lanes, district heating, or charging infrastructure.
determinants.mechanism: Infrastructure spending provides salient, widely shared benefits and low-carbon alternatives to taxed behaviours, enhancing perceived fairness and practicality of the tax.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon and fuel tax proposals where revenues fund public transport, cycling ways, heat grids, charging networks and similar environmental infrastructure.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support/WTP for carbon or fuel taxes when revenues fund environmental infrastructure.
determinants.evidence_quote: “Investments in environmental infrastructure… [are] recognized in many studies as a popular or the most popular recycling option.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Joint top position in consensus ordinal ranking (score 8).

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues for climate change adaptation spending
determinants.definition: Allocating carbon/fuel tax revenues to adaptation measures such as flood protection or other climate-resilience investments.
determinants.mechanism: Links tax revenues to visible climate-related benefits, but adaptation may feel less directly connected to emissions reductions than mitigation subsidies, so gains in acceptability are smaller.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax designs in several countries where revenues are earmarked for climate adaptation programmes.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon taxes with revenues devoted to adaptation versus other green or non-green uses.
determinants.evidence_quote: “Earmarking revenue for climate change adaptation… [is] found to be less popular than subsidies to low-carbon technologies and energy efficiency.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Intermediate position in ranking (score 2), above general budget and corporate tax cuts.
determinants.notes: Evidence based on relatively few studies.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues to fund environmental research and innovation
determinants.definition: Earmarking carbon/fuel tax revenues for R&D and technological innovation related to climate and energy.
determinants.mechanism: Some citizens value long-term innovation benefits, but impacts are less tangible and immediate; hence effects on acceptability vary and can be statistically insignificant.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon and fuel tax proposals where revenues finance environmental or energy-related R&D programmes.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon or fuel taxes when revenues fund research and innovation.
determinants.evidence_quote: “Funding research and technical innovation is a quite popular revenue recycling option… although one study shows it does not significantly affect public acceptability.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Considered in only three studies; one finds no significant effect on acceptability.
determinants.notes: Not included in the paper’s consensus ordinal ranking due to few studies.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Targeted transfers (social cushioning) to low-income and vulnerable households
determinants.definition: Using carbon/fuel tax revenues for targeted transfers or benefits to low-income or otherwise vulnerable households to offset higher energy costs.
determinants.mechanism: Improves perceived distributional fairness and protects vulnerable groups but can be seen as complex or unfair by others, leading to heterogeneous acceptance across studies.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax proposals with progressive revenue recycling via targeted transfers or benefits to low-income and vulnerable groups.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support/WTP for carbon taxes when revenues are used for targeted social cushioning.
determinants.evidence_quote: “This revenue recycling option has generally demonstrated a moderate effect… a closer look into the literature reveals mixed evidence.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Consensus ranking score 2; generally above lump-sum transfers but below top green earmarking options.
determinants.notes: Some studies find limited or even negative effects on acceptability.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Uniform lump-sum transfers (carbon dividends) to all households
determinants.definition: Recycling carbon/fuel tax revenues as equal per-capita cash transfers or rebates to all households or taxpayers.
determinants.mechanism: Can increase salience of compensation and progressivity, but many citizens still dislike the tax or distrust the scheme; support varies strongly across contexts and survey designs.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Fee-and-dividend style carbon tax proposals in multiple countries, with equal rebates or cheques to households.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support/WTP for carbon taxes with per-capita lump-sum rebates.
determinants.evidence_quote: “The impact of lump-sum transfers on public acceptability exhibits high variability… there is substantial evidence that lump-sum transfers are unpopular.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Consensus ranking score −6; among the least preferred options overall despite being most popular in some individual studies.
determinants.notes: Real-world evidence from Canada and Switzerland shows only limited impact on acceptance.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Tax rebates or compensation to affected economic sectors
determinants.definition: Using carbon/fuel tax revenues to provide tax rebates or compensation to firms or workers in sectors adversely affected by the tax (e.g. coal mining).
determinants.mechanism: Compensating firms can be seen as unfair or regressive, while compensating workers facing job losses can attract sympathy; overall acceptability depends on who benefits.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax designs where revenues are used to refund firms or support employees in carbon-intensive sectors.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon taxes with sectoral rebates versus other revenue uses.
determinants.evidence_quote: “Refunding firms does not garner significant support. However, the public acceptability of supporting employees… is relatively high.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Consensus ranking score 2; intermediate preference, above deficit reduction and general budget.
determinants.notes: Public tends to distinguish sharply between support for firms and for workers.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Reducing indirect taxes, such as VAT
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues to reduce indirect taxes (e.g. VAT)
determinants.definition: Recycling carbon/fuel tax revenues by cutting broad-based indirect taxes like value-added tax or similar consumption levies.
determinants.mechanism: Some view indirect-tax cuts as fair and efficiency-enhancing, while others doubt environmental effectiveness or do not link them to climate goals, leading to heterogeneous acceptance.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Environmental tax reforms where a carbon or fuel tax is combined with reductions in VAT or similar indirect taxes.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon or fuel taxes when paired with indirect-tax reductions.
determinants.evidence_quote: “Using carbon pricing revenues to reduce indirect taxes… yields mixed effects on acceptability. While some studies identify… unpopular… other studies indicate that reducing VAT rate is the most… preferred revenue recycling options.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Ranks third in consensus ordinal ranking (score 3), but individual studies range from least to most preferred.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Reducing labour taxes (including personal income taxes and social contributions)
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues to reduce labour taxes
determinants.definition: Recycling carbon/fuel tax revenues by lowering personal income taxes and/or payroll/social security contributions.
determinants.mechanism: Economists stress efficiency gains, but many citizens distrust promised tax swaps or underestimate behavioural responses, leading to modest or even negative effects on acceptability.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Environmental tax reforms combining carbon or energy taxes with reductions in income tax or social contributions in several European countries and survey scenarios elsewhere.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support/WTP for carbon or fuel taxes when revenues reduce labour taxes.
determinants.evidence_quote: “The evidence regarding the effect of reducing (personal) income taxes on public acceptability is mixed… Concerning reducing social contributions, studies found low… intermediate… and relatively high levels of public acceptability.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Consensus ranking score 1; generally less preferred than green earmarking and indirect-tax cuts, but above deficit reduction and general budget.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues to reduce corporate taxes
determinants.definition: Recycling carbon/fuel tax revenues by cutting business or corporate income taxes.
determinants.mechanism: Citizens tend to see corporate tax cuts as unfair, benefiting firms rather than households, and doubt that such use contributes to climate goals, leading to strong opposition.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Environmental tax reforms and carbon tax proposals where revenues are earmarked to reduce corporate taxation.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon or fuel taxes when revenues reduce corporate taxes.
determinants.evidence_quote: “The literature consistently identifies reducing corporate taxes as a highly unpopular recycling option.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Lowest position in consensus ordinal ranking (score −9).

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues for deficit or public debt reduction
determinants.definition: Allocating carbon/fuel tax revenues to reduce public debt or budget deficits rather than recycling to households or firms.
determinants.mechanism: Some value fiscal prudence, but many distrust politicians and dislike that revenues are not visibly returned or linked to climate benefits, limiting acceptability.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon and fuel tax proposals where revenues are earmarked for deficit or public debt reduction instead of tax cuts or transfers.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon taxes when revenues reduce public debt.
determinants.evidence_quote: “Some studies show that reducing public debt garners intermediate levels of acceptability… other studies reveal it is among the least popular options.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Consensus ranking score −4; more acceptable than general budget supplementation and corporate tax cuts.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: supplementing the government budget / general budget
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Using revenues to supplement the general government budget
determinants.definition: Allowing carbon/fuel tax revenues to flow into the general budget without specific earmarking for debt reduction or particular spending items.
determinants.mechanism: Low political trust and absent clear benefits create fears of hidden taxation and misuse, making this the least acceptable revenue option in most studies.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon and fuel tax scenarios where revenues are added to the general budget for discretionary spending.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon or fuel taxes when revenues supplement the general budget.
determinants.evidence_quote: “Using carbon pricing to supplement the government budget is highly unpopular compared to other recycling options… some studies identifying it as the least preferred option.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Consensus ranking score −7; among the two least preferred options.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: perceived fairness… with a specific emphasis on distributional fairness
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Perceived distributional fairness of carbon tax and revenue use
determinants.definition: Belief that the carbon or fuel tax and associated revenue recycling distribute burdens and benefits fairly across income groups and social categories.
determinants.mechanism: Policies perceived as fair—especially those protecting low-income households and imposing higher burdens on the affluent—are more legitimate, increasing willingness to accept or support the tax.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon and fuel tax proposals with varying incidence across income groups and different redistributive designs (e.g. progressive vs non-progressive recycling).
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support/WTP for carbon or fuel taxes as a function of perceived distributional fairness.
determinants.evidence_quote: “The literature demonstrates the significance of perceived fairness on the public acceptability of carbon pricing, with a specific emphasis on distributional fairness, particularly among income groups.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Review concludes that perceived fairness is a key driver; progressive designs are generally preferred.
determinants.notes: Includes preferences for higher burdens on affluent households and relief for low-income groups.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Carbon pricing policies can be regressive…
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Perceived regressivity and burden on low-income households
determinants.definition: Belief that carbon or fuel taxes impose a disproportionately large financial burden on low-income households relative to their income.
determinants.mechanism: Perceived regressivity undermines fairness and legitimacy, increasing opposition unless offset by progressive revenue recycling such as social cushioning or lump-sum transfers.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Analyses and survey descriptions of carbon and energy taxes that raise energy prices and may be regressive without compensatory transfers.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon or fuel taxes when they are seen as regressive versus when regressivity is mitigated.
determinants.evidence_quote: “Carbon pricing policies can be regressive, especially in the absence of any revenue redistribution… Addressing the potential regressivity… can be achieved through a progressive redistribution of revenues.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Review notes that without redistribution, regressivity can heavily disadvantage low-income households, harming acceptability.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Perceived environmental effectiveness of carbon taxes
determinants.definition: Belief that carbon or fuel taxes effectively reduce emissions and contribute meaningfully to environmental goals.
determinants.mechanism: When people think taxes substantially cut emissions, they see costs as justified and are more open to flexible revenue use; low perceived effectiveness fuels demands for earmarking and opposition.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax proposals where respondents receive or lack information about expected emissions reductions and environmental performance.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support/WTP for carbon taxes conditional on perceived effectiveness.
determinants.evidence_quote: “The preference for environmental earmarking is rooted in the perception that carbon pricing on its own is not effective to reduce carbon emissions… people hardly understand the incentives….”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Carattini et al. (2017) show that providing information on environmental effectiveness reduces preference for earmarking without lowering acceptability.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Political trust emerges as an important factor
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Political trust in government handling of carbon tax revenues
determinants.definition: Trust that politicians and government will implement carbon or fuel taxes honestly and use revenues as promised.
determinants.mechanism: High trust reduces fears that the tax is a revenue grab or that revenues will be misused, making citizens more willing to accept the tax and less insistent on strict earmarking.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Surveys and experiments on carbon and fuel taxes in multiple countries, examining how general political trust moderates support for such taxes and revenue uses.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon or fuel taxes across levels of political trust.
determinants.evidence_quote: “Political trust emerges as an important factor in explaining attitudes towards carbon pricing… those with high levels of political trust tend to be more positive towards carbon pricing.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Cites cross-national survey evidence (e.g. Fairbrother et al. 2019) showing stronger support among high-trust individuals.
determinants.notes: Low trust also strengthens preferences for strict earmarking over general budget use.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: salience and communication of revenue recycling
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Salience and information about revenue recycling
determinants.definition: Degree to which people are informed about and can clearly see how carbon tax revenues are used and what they receive in return.
determinants.mechanism: Clear, credible information about revenue use, transfers and emission reductions corrects misperceptions, raises perceived benefits and fairness, and can shift preferences toward progressive designs, increasing overall acceptability.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Survey experiments and real-world cases (Canada, Switzerland) where information about revenue recycling or its magnitude is or is not provided to respondents.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for carbon taxes before and after information on revenue recycling and its effects.
determinants.evidence_quote: “Providing information about revenue recycling increases public acceptability of carbon pricing… making the distributional effects salient… enhances the preference for progressive designs….”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Cites multiple experiments (e.g. Beiser-McGrath & Bernauer 2019; Jagers et al. 2021) showing positive information effects on support.
determinants.notes: Low baseline awareness of existing recycling in Canada and Switzerland highlights importance of salience.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: The carbon tax in British Columbia became acceptable over time
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Policy experience and learning from implemented carbon taxes
determinants.definition: Exposure to an actually implemented carbon tax over time, as opposed to hypothetical proposals only.
determinants.mechanism: As people observe real impacts and revenue recycling in practice, fears may subside and they adapt behaviour, leading to higher acceptance than before implementation.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: British Columbia’s revenue-neutral carbon tax introduced in 2008, with public opinion tracked over subsequent years.
determinants.outcome_variable: Public acceptability/support for an implemented carbon tax over time versus initial opposition pre-implementation.
determinants.evidence_quote: “Murray and Rivers (2015) observe that the carbon tax in British Columbia became acceptable over time, despite initial opposition before its implementation.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Cites longitudinal polling evidence from British Columbia indicating rising acceptance post-implementation.
